sn45.8:0.3: Sutta An Analysis of the Path
sn45.8:1.1: I have heard that at one time the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery. There he addressed the monks, “Monks!” “Yes, lord,” the monks responded to him. The Blessed One said,
sn45.8:1.2: “I will teach & analyze for you the noble eightfold path.
sn45.8:1.3: Listen & pay close attention. I will speak.”
sn45.8:1.4: “As you say, lord,” the monks responded to him.
sn45.8:2.1: “Now what, monks, is the noble eightfold path?
sn45.8:2.2: Right view, right resolve, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, right concentration.
sn45.8:3.2: Knowledge with regard to [or: in terms of] stress, knowledge with regard to the origination of stress, knowledge with regard to the stopping of stress, knowledge with regard to the way of practice leading to the stopping of stress:
sn45.8:3.3: This, monks, is called right view.
sn45.8:4.2: Resolve for renunciation, resolve for non-ill will, resolve for harmlessness:
sn45.8:4.3: This, monks, is called right resolve.
sn45.8:5.2: Abstaining from lying, abstaining from divisive speech, abstaining from harsh speech, abstaining from idle chatter:
sn45.8:5.3: This, monks, is called right speech.
sn45.8:6.2: Abstaining from taking life, abstaining from stealing, abstaining from sexual intercourse:
sn45.8:6.3: This, monks, is called right action.
sn45.8:7.2: There is the case where a disciple of the noble ones, having abandoned dishonest livelihood, keeps his life going with right livelihood.
sn45.8:7.3: This, monks, is called right livelihood.
sn45.8:8.2: (I) There is the case where a monk generates desire, endeavors, activates persistence, upholds & exerts his intent for the sake of the non-arising of evil, unskillful qualities that have not yet arisen.
sn45.8:8.3: (II) He generates desire, endeavors, activates persistence, upholds & exerts his intent for the sake of the abandoning of evil, unskillful qualities that have arisen.
sn45.8:8.4: (III) He generates desire, endeavors, activates persistence, upholds & exerts his intent for the sake of the arising of skillful qualities that have not yet arisen.
sn45.8:8.5: (IV) He generates desire, endeavors, activates persistence, upholds & exerts his intent for the maintenance, non-confusion, increase, plenitude, development, & culmination of skillful qualities that have arisen.
sn45.8:8.6: This, monks, is called right effort.
sn45.8:9.2: (I) There is the case where a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn45.8:9.3: (II) He remains focused on feelings in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn45.8:9.4: (III) He remains focused on the mind in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn45.8:9.5: (IV) He remains focused on mental qualities in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn45.8:9.6: This, monks, is called right mindfulness.
sn45.8:10.2: (I) There is the case where a monk—quite secluded from sensuality, secluded from unskillful qualities—enters & remains in the first jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of seclusion, accompanied by directed thought & evaluation.
sn45.8:10.3: (II) With the stilling of directed thoughts & evaluations, he enters & remains in the second jhāna: rapture & pleasure born of concentration, unification of awareness free from directed thought & evaluation—internal assurance.
sn45.8:10.4: (III) With the fading of rapture, he remains equanimous, mindful, & alert, and senses pleasure with the body. He enters & remains in the third jhāna, of which the noble ones declare, ‘Equanimous & mindful, he has a pleasant abiding.’
sn45.8:10.5: (IV) With the abandoning of pleasure & pain—as with the earlier disappearance of elation & distress—he enters & remains in the fourth jhāna: purity of equanimity & mindfulness, neither pleasure nor pain.
sn45.8:10.6: This, monks, is called right concentration.” That is what the Blessed One said. Gratified, the monks delighted in the Blessed One’s words.
